text:
AWS Bedrock and Available Foundation Models

What is Amazon Bedrock?
Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed AWS service that provides access to foundation models 
from multiple AI companies through a single API. You do not need to manage servers, 
download model weights, or handle infrastructure. You call the API and pay per token.

Key benefits of Bedrock:
- No GPU infrastructure to manage
- Access to models from Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Amazon, and others
- IAM-based access control integrates with existing AWS permissions
- Data does not leave your AWS account by default
- Supports fine-tuning and RAG workflows natively

Available model families on Bedrock:

- Supports fine-tuning and RAG workflows natively

Available model families on Bedrock:
Anthropic Claude Strong reasoning, long context, instruction following. 
Good for complex Q&A, summarization, and code. Claude 3 Haiku is fast and cheap. 
Claude 3 Sonnet balances speed and capability. Claude 3 Opus is the most capable.

Amazon Titan Amazon's own models. Titan Text for generation. 
Titan Embeddings V2 for creating 1024-dimensional embeddings. 
Tight integration with other AWS services.

Meta Llama Open weights model available through Bedrock. 
Llama 3 is strong for general purpose tasks. Good option when you want 
an open source model with managed infrastructure.

an open source model with managed infrastructure.

Mistral — European AI company. Fast models with strong multilingual support. 
Mistral Large competes with GPT-4 class models.

How to invoke a model via Bedrock:
You use the bedrock-runtime client in boto3. The invoke_model call takes a modelId, 
a JSON body formatted to that model's specification, and returns a response body. 
Each model family has a slightly different request and response format.

Model access:
Models must be explicitly enabled in the Bedrock console before use. Navigate to 
Amazon Bedrock, select Model Access, and request access to each model you need.

Amazon Bedrock, select Model Access, and request access to each model you need. 
Access is granted per region. Titan Embeddings V2 must be enabled in the same 
region your scripts are configured to use.

Bedrock vs SageMaker:
Bedrock is for using pre-built foundation models with no infrastructure work. 
SageMaker is for training, fine-tuning, and deploying custom models. For a RAG 
chatbot using existing models, Bedrock is the right choice.

Understanding Embeddings and Vector Search

What is an embedding?
An embedding is a numerical representation of text. When you pass a sentence to an 
embedding model, it returns a list of floating point numbers called a vector. Similar 
sentences produce vectors that are close together in mathematical space. Unrelated 
sentences produce vectors that are far apart.

Example:
"How do I restart the server?" and "What is the process for rebooting a machine?" 
would produce vectors very close to each other even though they share no exact words.

Why 1024 dimensions?
Amazon Titan Embeddings V2 produces 1024-dimensional vectors. This means each piece

Amazon Titan Embeddings V2 produces 1024-dimensional vectors. This means each piece 
of text becomes a list of 1024 numbers. More dimensions generally means more 
expressive power the model can encode more nuance about meaning. Other models 
use 768, 1536, or 3072 dimensions depending on their size and architecture.

What is a vector database?
A vector database is a system optimized for storing and searching embeddings. Unlike 
a traditional database that matches exact keywords, a vector database finds the nearest 
neighbors in high-dimensional space. FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, and Chroma are 
common options.

FAISS specifically:

common options.

FAISS specifically:
FAISS stands for Facebook AI Similarity Search. It is an open source library that stores 
vectors in memory and performs extremely fast similarity search. IndexFlatL2 uses 
Euclidean distance to find nearest neighbors. A lower L2 score means a closer match.

The chunking connection:
Documents must be split into chunks before embedding because embedding models have 
token limits. Each chunk gets its own embedding vector. When you search, you retrieve 
chunks not whole documents. This is why chunk size and overlap matter — they determine 
the granularity of what gets retrieved.

Semantic search vs keyword search:

the granularity of what gets retrieved.

Semantic search vs keyword search:
Traditional search matches exact words. Semantic search matches meaning. A RAG 
system using embeddings will find relevant chunks even when the user's question uses 
different vocabulary than the source document.

Large Language Model Fundamentals

What is a large language model?
A large language model (LLM) is a neural network trained on massive amounts of text 
data to predict and generate human language. Models like GPT-4, Claude, Llama, and 
Amazon Titan are all LLMs. They learn grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and style 
from the statistical patterns in their training data.

The transformer architecture:
All modern LLMs are built on the transformer architecture introduced in the 2017 paper 
"Attention is All You Need." The key innovation is the attention mechanism, which allows 
the model to weigh the importance of every word in the input relative to every other word

the model to weigh the importance of every word in the input relative to every other word 
when generating each output token.

Tokens not words:
LLMs process text as tokens, not words. A token is roughly 3-4 characters on average. 
The word "embedding" is one token. The word "unbelievable" might be split into two or 
three tokens. Token limits determine how much text you can send to a model in one call. 
GPT-4 supports up to 128,000 tokens. Claude 3 supports up to 200,000.

Temperature:
Temperature is a parameter that controls randomness in the model's output. A temperature 
of 0 makes the model deterministic it always picks the most likely next token. A

of 0 makes the model deterministic it always picks the most likely next token. A 
temperature of 1 introduces creativity and variation. For factual Q&A over documents, 
low temperature (0 to 0.3) is preferred.

Context window:
The context window is the total amount of text the model can see at once, including your 
prompt, retrieved chunks, and the conversation history. RAG systems must fit retrieved 
chunks within this window. If you retrieve too many chunks or chunks are too large, you 
will exceed the context limit.

Inference vs training:
Training is the expensive, one-time process of teaching the model on billions of tokens.

Training is the expensive, one-time process of teaching the model on billions of tokens. 
Inference is the cheaper, repeated process of using the trained model to generate responses. 
When you call Amazon Bedrock, you are doing inference you are not changing the model.

Hallucination:
Hallucination is when an LLM generates confident but false information. RAG reduces 
hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved documents. The model is more likely to 
stay accurate when relevant context is explicitly provided in the prompt.

Prompt Engineering Basics

What is prompt engineering?
Prompt engineering is the practice of crafting inputs to a language model to get 
better, more reliable outputs. Because LLMs are sensitive to how instructions are 
phrased, small changes in wording can produce significantly different results.

System prompts vs user prompts:
A system prompt sets the context, persona, and rules for the model before the 
conversation begins. A user prompt is the actual question or request. In a RAG 
system, the system prompt typically instructs the model to answer only based on 
provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the documents.

RAG prompt pattern:

RAG prompt pattern:
A standard RAG prompt injects retrieved chunks between the instruction and the 
question like this:

You are a helpful assistant. Answer the question using only the context below.
If the answer is not in the context, say you do not know.

Context:
[retrieved chunk 1]
[retrieved chunk 2]
[retrieved chunk 3]

Question: [user question]

This pattern keeps the model grounded and reduces hallucination significantly.

Few-shot prompting:
Few-shot prompting means including examples of the desired input-output format 
in the prompt itself. The model learns the pattern from the examples and applies

in the prompt itself. The model learns the pattern from the examples and applies 
it to new inputs. Zero-shot means no examples are given just the instruction.

Chain of thought:
Chain of thought prompting asks the model to reason step by step before giving 
a final answer. Adding "think step by step" or "explain your reasoning" to a 
prompt often improves accuracy on complex questions. This works because it forces 
the model to generate intermediate reasoning tokens that guide the final output.

Common mistakes:
- Vague instructions produce vague outputs. Be specific about format and length.
- Asking too many things in one prompt splits the model's attention.

- Asking too many things in one prompt splits the model's attention.
- Not providing context forces the model to rely on training data and hallucinate.
- Forgetting to tell the model what to do when it does not know the answer.

Prompt templates in RAG:
In production RAG systems, the prompt is assembled programmatically. The retriever 
finds the top chunks, the orchestrator inserts them into a template string, and the 
filled template is sent to the LLM. Lambda functions commonly handle this assembly 
step in AWS-based RAG architectures.

What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?

Retrieval-Augmented Generation, or RAG, is an AI architecture pattern that combines a 
language model with an external knowledge source. Instead of relying solely on what a 
model learned during training, RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and injects 
them into the prompt before the model generates a response.

Why RAG matters:
Large language models have a training cutoff date. They cannot know about events or 
documents created after they were trained. RAG solves this by giving the model access to 
a dynamic, updateable knowledge base at inference time.

How RAG works step by step:

a dynamic, updateable knowledge base at inference time.

How RAG works step by step:
1. Documents are split into chunks and converted into vector embeddings.
2. Embeddings are stored in a vector database such as FAISS or Pinecone.
3. When a user asks a question, the question is also converted into an embedding.
4. The vector database finds the most similar document chunks using distance math.
5. Those chunks are inserted into the LLM prompt as context.
6. The LLM generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content.

RAG vs fine-tuning:
Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into the model weights permanently. RAG keeps knowledge

Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into the model weights permanently. RAG keeps knowledge 
external and swappable. RAG is cheaper, faster to update, and easier to audit. Fine-tuning 
is better when you need to change the model's tone or behavior rather than its knowledge.

Common RAG use cases:
- Customer support bots grounded in product documentation
- Internal knowledge assistants over company wikis
- Legal or medical Q&A over curated document sets
- Code assistants with access to private repositories

RAG is the foundation of most production AI assistants deployed in enterprise settings today.
